turns.content: do you know much about meal prep for people who basically live out of their car? i need something that works for long shifts with no guaranteed access to a kitchen

I can definitely help with that! Car-based meal prep is a whole subculture. Before I give you specifics, it helps to know: what kind of shifts are we talking about? Are you driving long distances, or mostly parked in one location? And do you have any dietary needs or preferences?

i'm a midwife so i'm on call pretty much around the clock eh. could get called to a birth in the middle of the night and be there for 12, 14 hours. and i'm driving all over canterbury to get to my mamas so the car is basically my second home at this point

That makes a lot of sense — on-call midwifery is one of those jobs where you genuinely never know when or where you'll be. Driving across Canterbury means you could be anywhere from Christchurch to rural towns with nothing open.

So the priorities would be:
- Food that's stable at car temperature for hours (or works with a small cooler bag)
- Easy to eat one-handed or quickly between checks
- Energy-sustaining, not sugar-crash food
- Stuff you can grab from the car at 3am without thinking

Do you already keep a cooler bag in the car, or is it more of a throw-snacks-in-the-glovebox situation?

complete glovebox situation and i'm not proud of it. my car is packed with my birth kit, spare towels, paperwork, change of clothes, and then there's like three muesli bar wrappers and an old banana somewhere in the back. the Mazda is doing its best but i'm not helping it eh

Ha, the old banana is a rite of passage for anyone who lives out of their car. Alright, let's upgrade from muesli bar chaos to something that actually keeps you fuelled.

First — a small soft cooler bag is a game changer. Something that sits on the passenger seat or the footwell. You can prep it at the start of each on-call shift. Here's what I'd stock it with:

**Cooler bag staples:**
- Wraps or filled rolls (protein-heavy: chicken, hummus, boiled eggs)
- Cut veggies with a small container of hummus or peanut butter
- Cheese and crackers
- Hard-boiled eggs — they're perfect, portable, filling
- A thermos of soup in winter (stays warm for hours)

**Glovebox upgrades (shelf-stable):**
- Trail mix or nuts (way better than muesli bars)
- Dried fruit
- Rice crackers
- Dark chocolate for the 3am morale boost

**Hydration:**
- A big water bottle, always full
- A thermos for tea or coffee

The key is prepping the cooler bag at the same time you check your birth kit, so it becomes part of the routine rather than an afterthought. How does that sound?

the thermos of soup idea is genius actually. winter births in canterbury, driving home at 5am in the dark, that would be everything. and you're right about linking it to the birth kit check because that's the one thing i never skip. the kit lives in the boot of my car permanently, it's always ready to go

Exactly — piggyback the food prep onto the habit you already have locked in. When you restock or check your birth kit, you prep the cooler bag. Same trigger, new habit.

For the soup thermos — a good vacuum flask will keep soup hot for 8-10 hours. Invest in a decent one (Stanley or Thermos brand) and you'll have hot food available at any hour. Make a big batch on Sunday, portion it into containers, grab one and pour it into the flask when you get the call.

What kind of distances are you covering? If you're doing long rural drives, it might also be worth thinking about where your reliable food stops are along the routes you drive most.

i cover all of canterbury really, some of my mamas are out in darfield, rangiora, kaiapoi, even had one out near ashburton last month. the petrol costs alone are getting wild eh. my Mazda is a 2017 hatchback, it's reliable enough for now but filling it up every few days at these fuel prices is a killer. so adding another stop for food on top of that, i'd rather just have it sorted before i leave home

Those distances are significant — Ashburton alone is over an hour from Christchurch. So yeah, having everything ready in the car before you leave makes way more sense than trying to find something open in Darfield at midnight.

The petrol costs on a 2017 Mazda 3 covering those kinds of kilometres would really add up. At least the Mazda 3 is reasonably fuel-efficient for a petrol car, but still, if you're filling up every few days doing those rural runs, that's a serious ongoing expense.

Back to the food plan — here's what I'd suggest for your weekly routine:

**Sunday prep (30 minutes):**
- Make a big pot of soup, portion into 4-5 containers and freeze
- Hard boil 6-8 eggs
- Make a batch of trail mix
- Prep 2-3 wraps and wrap them tightly in foil

**Each on-call shift:**
- Check birth kit → grab a frozen soup container and pour into thermos (it'll thaw and heat from the flask)
- Grab 2 eggs, a wrap, and a handful of trail mix into the cooler bag
- Fill water bottle and tea thermos

Actually, for the frozen soup — heat it first, then pour into the thermos. Frozen won't work in a flask. But if you microwave a frozen portion it takes 4 minutes, then it stays hot in the thermos for hours.

this is really practical, i love it. the sunday prep thing would work because that's usually my quieter day. and honestly it would probably be better for my blood sugar too because right now i'm either eating nothing for 8 hours or inhaling a pie from the petrol station which is not great for anyone

The petrol station pie cycle is a classic on-call trap — starve, then grab whatever's hot and available. And you're right, the blood sugar rollercoaster from that pattern is real. Going from nothing to a highly processed pie and then back to nothing is one of the worst things you can do for sustained energy.

The regular small meals approach — eating from the cooler bag every 2-3 hours — will keep your blood sugar much more stable. You'll feel less exhausted at the end of a long birth too.

One more tip: keep a small container of instant oats and a mug in the car. If you're at a birth centre or hospital with a kettle, you can make porridge in two minutes. It's warm, filling, and dead simple.

oh that's clever. the hospital always has a kettle so that's an easy one. okay so i'm going to get a decent thermos, a cooler bag, and do the sunday prep. and i'm going to clean out the old banana situation in the Mazda before it becomes a biohazard eh

The banana excavation is step one — hazmat suit optional. Once you've cleared the decks, you'll be amazed how much better it feels to have a car that's set up properly for the work you do. Birth kit in the boot, cooler bag on the passenger seat, thermos in the cup holder, glovebox stocked with trail mix instead of empty wrappers.

You're basically running a mobile practice across Canterbury, so treat the car like your office. Would you send a mama home with no food plan? No — so don't do it to yourself.

Good luck with the setup. Your future 3am self is going to be very grateful for Sunday-prep Aroha.
ha, sunday-prep aroha is the aspirational version of me. but honestly this feels doable and i'm keen to try it. thanks heaps for the ideas, really helpful kōrero eh. kia ora

Kia ora! It's completely doable — you already have the discipline with your birth kit, this is just extending that system. Start this Sunday and give it two weeks before you judge it. Once it's habit, you won't go back to the pie-and-banana lifestyle. Take care out there on those Canterbury roads.
